segments.s763-g25: به ایران is a prepositional phrase placed up front for emphasis (literally 'to/for Iran' here meaning 'in Iran' or 'for life in Iran'). تو را uses the object/beneficiary pronoun with the enclitic را (object/topic marker) after تو; this marks 'you' as the one for whom the statement holds. زندگانی بس است is a verbless/predicative nominal clause with بس 'enough' and است 'is' as the copula: 'a life is enough (for you)'.
segments.s763-g26: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'), linking this clause to the previous sentence. تاج و نگین is a simple coordination ('crown and gem/signet'). بهر functions as a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to the benefit of'. دیگر کس literally 'another person' (دیگر 'other/another' + کس 'person/someone'). است is the copula completing the clause: 'are for another person.'
segments.s763-g27: که begins a relative/explanatory clause ('who/that'). با + noun marks accompaniment or possession here ('with' or 'having'): با پیل 'with elephants'. گنجست is a poetic contraction of گنج است ('is/has treasure') — an archaic fused form common in epic meter, so read as 'and had treasure'. و ... با فروجاه continues the list ('and with marketplaces' or 'and market-stalls').
segments.s763-g28: پدر بر پدر is a parallel/reduplicative phrase meaning 'father upon father' — i.e., generation after generation or 'father succeeded father'; here پدر is used in the sense 'ancestor/forefather'. بر is the preposition 'upon/over' indicating succession or stacking of fathers. نامبردار is an adjective 'renowned' describing شاه 'king'. The sequence thus says that, from father to father, he was the famed king (or the king was famed through successive fathers).
segments.s763-g29: به دیدار او is a locative/temporal expression meaning 'at his sight / in his presence' (به + noun + pronoun). بر فلک places the following predicate 'upon the heavens/sky'. ماه نیست is a negated existential/copular clause: 'the moon does not exist (there)' — the whole line poetically says the moon is nothing in view of him.
segments.s763-g30: به بالای او 'on/at his height' (به + بالای + pronoun) expresses exalted rank or loftiness. بر زمین 'on the earth' limits the domain. شاه نیست is again a negated copular/existential clause: 'there is no king.' Together: on his high station there is no king upon earth (i.e., none comparable).
segments.s763-g31: هر آن گه که is a fixed temporal clause meaning 'whenever' or 'each time that' (هر + آن + گه + که). در بزم 'in the banquet/assembly' is the locative phrase. خندان is an adjective 'smiling/cheerful' and شود is the verb 'becomes/happens' — together: 'whenever it happens that (he) is smiling in the banquet.'
segments.s763-g32: گشاده لب is a nominal phrase with adjective before noun ('open-lipped' / 'with broad lips'), here describing a smiling mouth. و links to سیم دندان, a descriptive compound meaning 'silver-toothed' (سیم 'silver' modifying دندان 'tooth'); شود repeats the verb 'becomes' or 'appears' — 'his lips become wide and his teeth [become] silver-like', i.e. he beams with wide smile and bright (silver) teeth.
segments.s763-g41: سگ و یوز او is the subject phrase “his dogs and cheetahs” (possessive او follows the nouns). بیشتر زان is a comparative phrase “more than that” (بیشتر = more; زان = ‘than that / from that’), and خورد is the past-tense verb “ate.” Word order is Subject + comparative phrase + verb, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s763-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause (“for / because / in that”) with شاه as the subject. آن refers back to the things just mentioned (“those”), به چیزی is the idiom “as anything / worth anything,” and همی‌نشمرد is an archaic negative imperfect/past meaning “did not reckon / did not count” (همی marks continuous/habitual aspect). The clause thus says the king did not regard them as anything.
segments.s763-g43: شما را is a dative/topic-marked address “to you / as for you” (را here marks the person affected). به دیده درون is an adverbial phrase ‘in the inward eye / inward sight’ (به + دیده + درون), and شرم نیست is an existential negative: ‘shame does not exist.’ Together: “You have no inward shame.”
segments.s763-g44: ز راه خرد is a prepositional phrase ‘from / by the way of wisdom’ (ز = ‘from’, راه خرد = ‘the way of wisdom’). مهر و آزرم lists two qualities ‘affection (مهر) and آزرم’ and نیست negates their existence (‘there is none’). The phrase says that along the path of wisdom there is neither affection nor (archaic) modesty/honor.
segments.s763-g45: بدان is an imperative-clause marker here: ب‑ is the verbal prefix and دان (from دانستن) = “know,” so together بد ان = “know that.” The following coordinated noun phrase چهره و زاد و آن مهر و خوی lists the items of which the listener should take notice (face, lineage/birth, and that love/temperament); Persian has no overt case marking, so these nouns are simply the objects/complements of the introductory verb 'know'.
segments.s763-g46: چنین is a premodifier meaning “such/so,” modifying the compound noun تاج و تخت (“crown and throne”). آمدت is the past tense آمد plus the enclitic pronoun ‑ت “to/for you,” here meaning “came to you” or “befell you”; آرزوی (a wish) is the noun that functions as the thing that ‘came’ (typical Persian order: verb + indirect object + postverbal indefinite subject). The clause therefore says that a wish for such crown and throne came/occurred to you.
segments.s763-g47: جهان‌گر is an agentive/addressing term meaning “(one) who loves/seeks the world” (here a vocative or conditional address to a worldly person). بر اندازه جویی combines بر + اندازه (“with/in measure/moderation”) and جویی (present/imperf. of جستن ‘seek’), with همی marking habitual/continuous aspect — “seek with measure” or “seek in moderation.”
segments.s763-g48: سخن بر گزافه = “words in exaggeration/overstatement” (سخن = speech/words; گزافه = exaggerated). نگویی is the negative present/imperfect form (ن + گویی) of گفتن/گوییدن, here meaning “do not say/utter,” and the final همی again gives habitual/imperative coloring — a general injunction not to speak in exaggeration.
segments.s763-g65: نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (root meaning 'look/heed') plus the auxiliary/imperative کن ('do!') = 'look/pay attention'. بدین نامه پندمند is the object phrase: بدین (به + این, 'to/at this') + نامه ('letter') with the adjective پندمند ('admonitory, full of counsel') modifying نامه; the adjective follows the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s763-g66: مکن is the prohibitive 'do not' placed before the verb phrase. چشم و گوش و خرد را is the definite object marked by را (the three nouns joined with و). ببند is the singular imperative of بستن ('to close'); together: 'Do not close the eyes, the ears, and the mind/wisdom.' خرد here means 'reason/wisdom'.
segments.s763-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. نامه is the subject. به مهر اندر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'came/arrived with (a) seal' (به used here like 'by/with', مهر = 'seal', اندر آمد = 'came in/entered'). به داد is the verb phrase indicating giving: به marks the indirect object relation and داد is the past 'gave' (the recipient appears in the following segment).
segments.s763-g68: به marks the indirect object 'to'. پیروز شاپور فرخ نژاد is a sequence of epithets/names: پیروز ('victorious' as epithet), شاپور (personal name), فرخ نژاد ('fortunate-born' = 'Farrokh‑nejad', a family/lineage epithet). Together they name the recipient of the letter.
segments.s763-g69: بر سعد places the opposing/target phrase first: بر here indicates 'against' or 'over' Sa'd. وقاص is the subject (the name Waqas). شد پهلوان is the predicate 'became a champion' (شد = 'became', پهلوان = 'champion/hero'). The poetic word order fronts the object/scene before the subject and verb.
segments.s763-g70: از ایران is a prepositional source/partitive 'from/of Iran'. بزرگان روشن‌روان is a noun phrase 'the great ones (بزرگان) described as روشن‌روان' ('bright‑souled' or 'clear‑minded'); روشن‌روان is an epithet modifying بزرگان.
segments.s763-g71: همه غرقه is a predicative phrase 'all (were) drenched/immersed'. در introduces the medium 'in' and is followed by a list of items: جوشن (cuirass/armor) و سیم و زر ('and silver and gold'). The whole phrase describes their being richly armed and covered in metal.
segments.s763-g72: سپرهای زرین is 'golden shields' (سپرهای = plural 'shields', زرین = 'golden'). و introduces a second item, زرین کمر, 'golden belts/girdles' (کمر here = 'girdle/belt'). The repetition of زرین emphasizes richness; adjectives follow the nouns as usual.
segments.s763-g1: A noun phrase with an adverb and a simile: فرستاده (the messenger) is followed by نیز ('too/also') for emphasis; سپس 'چون برق و رعد' is a simile ('like lightning and thunder') that modifies the manner of the action to come. There is no copula — the phrase functions as the subject + manner adjunct before the verb.
segments.s763-g2: فرستاد is the simple past verb 'sent' (he sent). تازان is the direct object ('the raiders'/'swift horsemen'); the prepositional phrase به نزدیک سعد ('toward/near Saʿd') gives direction. Word order is somewhat flexible/poetic here (verb appears before its object and the directional phrase follows).
segments.s763-g3: یکی ('a certain') introduces an indefinite; نامه‌ای is the indefinite object 'a letter' (with the -ای ending); بر حریر سپید is a locative phrase 'on white silk' (بر + noun + adjective). The whole chunk names the physical letter and its material.
segments.s763-g4: نویسنده is the agent 'the scribe/writer' and بنوشت here functions as the past tense verb 'wrote' (an older/poetic form related to نوشت). تابان چو شید is a postverbal descriptive phrase: تابان 'radiant/shining' modified by چو شید 'like the shid/sun' — a simile qualifying the scribe or the way he wrote. (Some lexemes in the provided sense lists do not match the classical senses here, see senses marked -1.)
segments.s763-g5: به عنوان بر is a fixed phrase meaning 'under the title/heading' or 'inscribed as'; از پور هرمزد شاه is a source phrase 'from Pour‑Hormozd the king' where پور is a name element. The whole segment gives the formal heading of the letter (who it is from).
segments.s763-g6: An appositive title and name: جهان‌پهلوان is a compound title 'world‑champion' placed before the proper name رستم; نیک‌خواه is an epithet 'well‑wisher' or 'benevolent' that follows the name. This segment thus names and titles the sender or signatory.
segments.s763-g7: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward(s)', followed by the full personal name سعد وقاص. جوینده جنگ is an epithet 'seeker of war' (جوینده modifying جنگ) placed after the name as a descriptive tag.
segments.s763-g8: This is a resultative/participial clause: جهان کرده ('having made the world' / 'he had made the world') with بر خویشتن ('upon himself') indicating the effect on the subject; تار و تنگ is a fixed pair meaning 'tight and narrow' or 'straitened'. Together the phrase describes a state he brought about for himself (literally 'he had made the world tight and narrow upon himself').
segments.s763-g49: This is an imperative request: 'سخن گوی مردی بر ما فرست' literally orders someone to 'send to us a man who is a speaker.' 'سخن گوی' is a noun compound (word order: noun + modifier) meaning 'speaker' or 'spokesman.' 'بر ما فرست' is verb + indirect object: فرست (imperative 'send') takes 'بر ما' ('toward us/to us') as the beneficiary.
segments.s763-g50: This repeats the verb 'فرست' and lists adjectives describing the person to be sent. 'جهاندیده' and 'زیبا' are attributive adjectives (worldly‑wise, beautiful). 'گرد' here has the verbal/adjectival sense 'one who goes about' (well‑travelled/versatile). The final 'فرست' is an echoing imperative (repeat for emphasis): 'send (him).' Word order is adjective1 + و + adjective2 + و + adjective3 + verb.
segments.s763-g51: 'بدان' is an imperative/hortative built from the prefix ب- + دان (here functioning as 'know' / 'inform'), meaning 'let him know' or 'inform (him).' 'تا' introduces purpose/result ('so that/in order that') and is followed by 'بگوید', a third‑person subjunctive (may say / might say). The clause 'که رای تو چیست' is a dependent content clause introduced by 'که' ('what/that') with 'رای تو' as subject and 'چیست' as the copula 'is.'
segments.s763-g52: This is a short interrogative nominal phrase: 'به تخت کیان' ('to/for the throne of the Kians') is a prepositional phrase marking the domain, and 'رهنمای تو کیست' is a copular question 'who is your guide.' Word order places the prepositional phrase before the predicate; 'کیست' is the copula used to ask 'who is...'.
segments.s763-g53: 'سواری فرستیم' uses the first‑person plural form فرستیم in a hortatory/optative sense 'let us send.' The noun سواری ('rider/horseman') is the object; 'نزدیک شاه' is a locative/prepositional phrase ('near the king') following the verb phrase.
segments.s763-g54: This has two coordinated clauses. 'بخواهیم ازو' continues the hortatory sense 'let us request/ask from him' (بخواهیم = 'let us ask'). The second clause 'هرچ خواهی بخواه' is an imperative addressed to 'you': 'هرچ خواهی' = 'whatever you desire' (خواهی = 2nd person 'you wish'), and 'بخواه' is the imperative 'ask/request.'
segments.s763-g55: This is an admonition: 'تو' (you) is topicalized, 'جنگ' gives the setting 'in war,' and 'چنان پادشاهی' ('such kingship') is the object. 'مجوی' is a negative imperative (archaic/poetic) meaning 'do not seek' — the verb form is a prohibitive second‑person marker.
segments.s763-g56: 'که' introduces the result/consequence clause ('for/that') and 'فرجام کارانده' is a nominal phrase in which 'فرجام' = 'outcome/end' is modified by 'کارانده' (a participial/adjectival form meaning 'bringing about' or 'causing'). The verbs 'آید' (comes) and 'بروی' (goes upon / befalls you) form a verbal sequence indicating that this doom will come upon you; both are present‑tense forms used to state a likely/resulting event.
segments.s763-g17: به پیش آمد is a poetic way to say ‘happened’ or ‘befell’; here آمد (used in the sense “happen”) is the main verb and پیش is part of the idiom پیش آمدن. The noun phrase این ناپسندیده کار is the thing that ‘happened’ (subject/absolute phrase); word order is slightly marked for poetry (verb phrase before the nominal phrase).
segments.s763-g18: به بیهوده here functions adverbially (‘to no avail / in vain’) and scopes over the following noun phrase; there is no explicit copula, so the phrase reads as a nominal predicate 'this suffering and this battle (are) in vain'. کارزار and رنج are coordinated by و and both are pointed to by the preceding demonstrative این.
segments.s763-g19: به من بازگوی is an imperative/request pattern ‘tell (to) me’ (بازگوی is the poetic imperative/imperative-like form of بازگفتن/بازگو کردن). آنک introduces the relative clause 'who/that', and شاه تو کیست is the embedded question with the copula ست (is). The possessive relation ‘your king’ is expressed by putting شاه before تو.
segments.s763-g20: This is a series question: چه modifies the coordinated nouns مردی و آیین و راه, and the copula (ست / چیست) at the end applies to them with تو as the subject. The construction asks for classification/description (‘what kind of man’ / ‘what custom and way’ you have); word order places the interrogative phrase before the final copula.
segments.s763-g21: به نزد is a prepositional phrase ‘to the presence/of someone’; که introduces the indirect question 'to whom' and jویی (from جستن/جویی) is the verb 'you seek/you come looking for'. همی is a poetic/progressive marker attached to the verb (emphasizing ongoing or customary action). دستگاه here means 'retinue/entourage/court apparatus' and is the object of جویی.
segments.s763-g22: Two terse noun phrases, each with the adjective برهنه placed before the noun for emphasis: 'a commander stripped / an army stripped.' There is no explicit verb — a common poetic compression that implies 'is' or describes their state. سپهبد is a titled military commander (spahbed) and سپاه is 'army, host'.
segments.s763-g23: به نانی is an instrumental/limiting phrase 'with a piece of bread / on a morsel of bread.' The clause تو سیری و هم گرسنه uses two predicates (سیری = 'sated' / state of satiety, and گرسنه = 'hungry') joined by و هم to produce an oxymoronic or paradoxical description—a poetic way to indicate scarcity and precariousness; the copula is omitted.
segments.s763-g24: This is a negative list using repeated نه ... و نه ... (neither ... nor ...): 'neither elephant nor throne nor baggage nor bundle.' The items enumerate prestigious or practical possessions they lack; بنه here means a small bundle or pack (not the pistachio).
segments.s763-g9: سرنامه is the book's prefatory heading or preface as the speaking source; گفت is the verb 'said' (classical/past narration used to introduce a quotation); از here functions as a preposition meaning 'concerning' or 'of'; جهاندار is a title 'sovereign' and پاک is an adjective 'pure' modifying it. Word order follows classical Persian: source + گفت + از + noun phrase.
segments.s763-g10: بباید is a modal 'must/ought' (classical present/modal use); که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of the speech; باشیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive ('we should / we be') rather than an imperative; با + (بیم و باک) is a prepositional phrase 'with fear and dread' — بیم و باک is an idiomatic paired synonym ('fear and dread/care'), linked by و 'and'.
segments.s763-g11: کزویست is a contracted classical phrase (کز وی است = 'it is from him / because of him'); بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; پای here means 'foot/base' (figurative base or support); گردان is an adjective 'revolving/turning' modifying سپهر, and سپهر is the poetic word for 'heavens/sky'. The phrase thus gives a causal statement and an inverted, poetic order: 'it is from him that the revolving heavens rest on a base.'
segments.s763-g12: همه + پادشاهیش = 'all / the whole of his kingship' (پادشاهی + ـش possessive = 'his rule'); دادست is an archaic/canonical copular/nominal phrase (داد است) here meaning 'is justice/right' or 'is given as justice' rather than a simple active past 'gave'; و links the next noun; مهر here means 'kindness / favour' (a quality of his rule). Overall: 'all his rule is (made of) justice and kindness.'
segments.s763-g13: ازو is 'from him/of him' (referring back to the divine or earlier referent); باد is the optative particle 'may' (a classical Persian wish/benediction); بر is the preposition 'upon'; شهریار is 'king/sovereign'; آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' rather than the verb 'to create'. The phrase is a blessing: 'May praise from him be upon the sovereign.'
segments.s763-g14: که introduces the reason/explanation 'for' or 'because'; زیبای is an adjectival form 'beautiful' placed before the noun (poetic ordering); تاجست is 'tāj + -ast' (noun + copula) = 'is a crown' or 'the crown is'; و links items in a triad; تخت is 'throne' and نگین 'gem'. The clause gives the justification: 'for the crown and throne and gem are beautiful.'
segments.s763-g15: که is a relative/complementizer 'who/that'; دارد is the present tense 'has/possesses' (here functioning as 'has the power/means'); به + فر = 'by/with (his) far' — فر is 'royal glory' or divine splendour, this phrase marks the instrument or means ('by his glory'); اهرمن is the proper name Ahriman (the devil); راببند is a subjunctive/jussive verb form 'bind (him)' with the object marker را attached to the verb (را+ببند → راببند), expressing the ability/wish to bind Ahriman. The construction implies 'he has (such) glory to bind Ahriman.'
segments.s763-g16: خداوند here functions as 'the Lord' or 'sovereign' (an honorific/epithet, appositive or title); شمشیر و تاج بلند is a coordinated noun phrase 'sword and lofty/high crown' where بلند is an adjective modifying تاج. Together this names (or apposes) the lord as 'lord of the sword and the lofty crown' or evokes the martial and regal attributes associated with the lord.
segments.s763-g57: This is a stacked nominal phrase in apposition: نبیره (a scion/heir) is followed by two epithets. Classical Persian poetry often omits the connecting ezafe; جهاندار here names the sovereign from whom he descends, and نوشین روان is a compounded epithet ('nushin‑ravān' = 'sweet‑soul' / 'pleasant‑soul'). Word order is noun + title + adjectival epithet, all describing the same person.
segments.s763-g58: که is a relative pronoun 'who/which/that' linking this clause to the person just named. با is the instrumental 'by/with', introducing the means; داد is an abstract noun 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'); او is the 3rd‑person clitic 'his/he'. پیرگردد is a third‑person verb (گردد) with پیر as the predicative element — poetically rendered here as 'is turned from old into young' (گردیدن = 'to become/turn'); جوان is the predicate complement 'young'. The clause thus means 'who, by his justice, makes old become young.'
segments.s763-g59: پدر بر پدر is a repetitive idiom 'father upon father' (i.e. generation upon generation / successive fathers/ancestors); بر carries the sense 'upon'. شاه names those fathers as kings. و خود شهریار places خود ('himself') before شهریار for emphasis ('and himself the sovereign/king'). The phrase is an appositive/epithet cluster describing continuous royal lineage and the present ruler.
segments.s763-g60: This clause is straightforward: زمانه is the subject 'the age/era', ندارد is the present negative of 'to have' ('does not have'), and چنو یادگار is the object 'such a memorial' (چنو = archaic/poetic variant of چنین 'such'). Word order is subject – verb – object (object phrase inverted for emphasis in poetry).
segments.s763-g61: جهانی مکن is a negative imperative: مکن 'do not do' applied to جهانی; here it functions as 'do not make (the) world/whole' or idiomatically 'do not make (the) world [so and so]'. پر ز + noun is a fixed pattern meaning 'full of (something)'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. نفرین is 'curse' and خوی + ش (enclitic) yields خویش 'your/one's own'. Together: 'Do not make the world full of your curse.'
segments.s763-g62: مشو is a negative imperative of شدن 'do not become'. بد گمان is a nominal/adjectival phrase 'of bad suspicion' = 'suspicious / having ill opinion'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within', آیین is 'custom/rite/practice', and خوی + ش again makes 'خویش' = 'your/one's own'. The clause therefore says 'Do not become distrustful about your own practices.'
segments.s763-g63: به تخت کیان literally 'at/to the throne of the Kians' (تخت + the dynasty name کیان). تا is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'so long as / while'. نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive of بودن 'to be' and here means 'is not lacking' or 'there is not' in the subordinate temporal clause; نژاد is 'lineage/descendants'. Together the phrase sets the temporal condition: 'while there is lineage upon the Kian throne.' (Poetic negation and word order are typical of Ferdowsi.)
segments.s763-g64: نجوید is a third‑person verbal form from جست‌و/جویدن 'to seek/find' (here used impersonally or with an implied subject) meaning 'will seek / will find / will be without' depending on context; خداوند is used as 'lord/sovereign' (an honorific/title), and فرهنگ و داد is the compound object 'learning/culture and justice'. In the whole line the clause conveys that, under the stated condition, the realm will not be without (or will possess) a sovereign who embodies culture and justice — i.e. 'there will be a lord of learning and justice.'
segments.s763-g33: The phrase names what is given: بخشد (he gives/bestows) with بهای سر تازیان as its object — “the price/reward (بها) of the Arabs' heads.” The initial به here functions as a prepositional/benefactive marker tied to the verbal action (classical word order places the verb near its objects). تازیان is plural “Arabs,” and بازاری/possessive relations are expressed by juxtaposing بهای + سر + تازیان.
segments.s763-g34: که introduces a result/purpose clause (“so that / that”), بر گنج او = “upon his treasure,” زان = “from that / from it” (referring to the act just mentioned), and نیاید is a negative jussive/subjunctive form meaning “may not/should not come.” زیان is the noun “harm/loss.” Together: “so that no loss should come upon his treasure from that.”
segments.s763-g35: This is an imperative request: a list of animals (سگ, یوز, بازش) is followed by the verb ده (“give”) and the numeral دو هزار (“two thousand”) — classical/poetic inversion places the objects before the verb and number. بازش is باز (hawk/falcon) plus the enclitic -ش “his.” The nouns are the things to be supplied in the quantity stated.
segments.s763-g36: که introduces a relative clause modifying the animals just named: با = “with/armed with.” زنگ = “bells” (ornaments that ring), زرند here refers to golden trappings/ornamentation, and گوشوار = “earrings/ornaments.” Repetition of با and و (and) lists the accoutrements that accompany the animals.
segments.s763-g37: به سالی = “in a year / within a year” (time frame). همه دشت = “the whole plain(s),” and نیزه‌وران is a compound meaning “spear‑bearers” (warriors armed with spears); together this names the people affected. Word order is classical: temporal phrase + whole-group + occupational description.
segments.s763-g38: نیابند is a negative plural form (here subjunctive/optative flavor) of یافتن ‘to find’ meaning “they will not find / cannot obtain.” خورد functions as the object meaning “(anything) to eat” / “food” (i.e. obtain to eat). از کران تا کران = “from edge to edge,” an idiom meaning “throughout / from end to end.”
segments.s763-g39: که begins the clause linking back to the previous statement. او را (with را marking the object/emphasis) indicates the beneficiary/affected person “for him.” باید is the modal “must / it is necessary,” and the repeated به before the nouns introduces the things required: به یوز و به سگ = “cheetahs and dogs” (i.e., he should be provided with them).
segments.s763-g40: که introduces the purpose/result: در دشت = “in the plain,” نخچیر = “game / prey,” and گیرد is the present/subjunctive of گرفتن “to seize/take” meaning “may catch/seize.” به تگ is a prepositional phrase where تگ (here) functions adverbially as “single / one‑by‑one” (i.e. catching prey singly), so the clause says he should have those animals so he can catch game in the plain in that manner.

sentences.s763-s13: A life in Iran is enough for you; the crown and the signet are for another person.
sentences.s763-s14: Who had elephants and treasure and marketplaces; father succeeded father, the famed king.
sentences.s763-s15: In his presence the moon is not upon the heavens; on his exalted height there is no king on the earth.
sentences.s763-s16: Whenever, in the smiling banquet, he appears, his lips are wide and his teeth are like silver.
sentences.s763-s21: His dogs and cheetahs ate more than that, for the king did not count those as anything.
sentences.s763-s22: You have no inward shame; by the path of wisdom there is neither affection nor modesty.
sentences.s763-s23: Know that by that face and birth and that love and disposition, such crown and throne became your desire.
sentences.s763-s24: O seeker of the world, seek in measure; do not utter exaggerated words.
sentences.s763-s33: Take heed of this admonitory letter; do not shut your eyes, ears, and reason.
sentences.s763-s34: When the letter came in sealed, it was given to the victorious Shapur Farrokh‑nejad.
sentences.s763-s35: Waqas became a champion against Sa'd; he was among Iran's great, bright‑souled men.
sentences.s763-s36: They were all drenched in armor and in silver and gold — golden shields and golden belts.
sentences.s763-s1: The messenger, too, like lightning and thunder, sent the raiders toward Saʿd.
sentences.s763-s2: A certain letter on white silk a radiant scribe wrote, shining like the sun.
sentences.s763-s3: Under the heading 'From Pour‑Hormozd the king' (was) 'Rostam, the world‑champion, the well‑wisher.'
sentences.s763-s4: Toward Saʿd Waqāṣ, the seeker of battle, (who) had made the world narrow and straitened upon himself.
sentences.s763-s25: Send us a spokesman, a man; send one who is worldly‑wise, well‑travelled, and handsome.
sentences.s763-s26: Inform him so that he may say what your counsel is; (and) who is your guide to the throne of the Kians?
sentences.s763-s27: Let us send a horseman near the king and ask of him; ask whatever you wish.
sentences.s763-s28: In war do not seek such kingship, for a fateful, harm‑bringing outcome may come upon you.
sentences.s763-s9: This unseemly deed happened, and this hardship and this battle were in vain.
sentences.s763-s10: Tell me who your king is; what sort of man you are and what your custom and way are.
sentences.s763-s11: To whose presence are you coming with your retinue? A spahbed uncovered, an army uncovered.
sentences.s763-s12: With a morsel of bread you are sated and yet hungry; no elephant, no throne, no baggage or bundle.
sentences.s763-s5: The preface said of the pure sovereign that we must be in fear and dread.
sentences.s763-s6: Because it is from him, the revolving heavens rest on a base; all his kingship is justice and kindness.
sentences.s763-s7: May praise from him be upon the king, for the crown and the throne and the jewel are beautiful.
sentences.s763-s8: who, by his (divine) glory, can bind Ahriman — the lord of the sword and the lofty crown.
sentences.s763-s29: A scion of the sovereign, sweet-souled, who by his justice turns old age into youth.
sentences.s763-s30: Father upon father a king, and himself a sovereign — the age has no such memorial.
sentences.s763-s31: Do not fill the world with your curses; do not become ill‑disposed (suspicious) within your own custom.
sentences.s763-s32: So long as the Kian throne has lineage, the realm will not lack a lord of learning and justice.
sentences.s763-s17: He gave the reward for the Arabs' heads, so that no loss should come upon his treasure from that.
sentences.s763-s18: Give him two thousand hounds, cheetahs, and falcons, each with bells and golden trappings and with earrings.
sentences.s763-s19: In the space of a year the spear‑bearers of the whole plain will not find anything to eat from one edge to the other.
sentences.s763-s20: That he must have cheetahs and dogs, so that in the plain he may seize game singly.
